ASXL1 truncation dysregulates Wnt signaling and tissue patterning

evidence.snippet: ASXL1 | HGNC:18318 | Bohring-Opitz syndrome | MONDO:0011510 | AD | Definitive

nodes.description: Truncating ASXL1 variants disrupt maintenance of developmental gene expression programs, including Hox gene regulation, and alter chromatin- associated transcriptional control across tissues.
nodes.id: ASXL1 truncation disrupts epigenetic regulation of developmental gene expression

biological_processes: regulation of transcription by RNA polymerase II

evidence.reference_title: De novo nonsense mutations in ASXL1 cause Bohring-Opitz syndrome.
evidence.snippet: We sequenced the exomes of three individuals with Bohring-Opitz syndrome and in each identified heterozygous de novo nonsense mutations in ASXL1, which is required for maintenance of both activation and silencing of Hox genes.

evidence.reference_title: Multiomics of Bohring-Opitz syndrome truncating ASXL1 mutations identify canonical and noncanonical Wnt signaling dysregulation.
evidence.snippet: ASXL1 (additional sex combs-like 1) plays key roles in epigenetic regulation of early developmental gene expression.

nodes.description: Multiomics studies in patient-derived cells show canonical and noncanonical Wnt pathway perturbation, including broad activation of canonical Wnt signaling and increased VANGL2 expression, consistent with disturbed tissue patterning during embryogenesis.
nodes.id: ASXL1 truncation dysregulates Wnt signaling and tissue patterning

biological_processes: Wnt signaling pathway, planar cell polarity pathway

evidence.reference_title: Multiomics of Bohring-Opitz syndrome truncating ASXL1 mutations identify canonical and noncanonical Wnt signaling dysregulation.
evidence.snippet: The data showed a broad activation of canonical Wnt signaling at the transcriptional and protein levels and upregulation of VANGL2, which encodes a planar cell polarity pathway protein that acts through noncanonical Wnt signaling to direct tissue patterning and cell migration.

nodes.description: The typical flexed elbow, wrist, and metacarpophalangeal posture is a defining BOS clinical sign.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical management of patients with ASXL1 mutations and Bohring-Opitz syndrome, emphasizing the need for Wilms tumor surveillance.
evidence.snippet: Bohring-Opitz syndrome is a rare genetic condition characterized by distinctive facial features, variable microcephaly, hypertrichosis, nevus flammeus, severe myopia, unusual posture (flexion at the elbows with ulnar deviation, and flexion of the wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints), severe intellectual disability, and feeding issues.

nodes.description: Poor postnatal weight gain and failure to thrive are common in BOS and are strongly influenced by feeding difficulty.

evidence.snippet: Feeding difficulties in early childhood, including cyclic vomiting, have a significant impact on overall health; feeding tends to improve with age.

nodes.description: Feeding intolerance, often with cyclic vomiting or aspiration, is a major early-life BOS complication.

evidence.snippet: Feeding difficulties in early childhood, including cyclic vomiting, have a significant impact on overall health; feeding tends to improve with age.

nodes.description: Global developmental delay is common in BOS and reflects pervasive neurodevelopmental impairment.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical management of patients with ASXL1 mutations and Bohring-Opitz syndrome, emphasizing the need for Wilms tumor surveillance.
evidence.snippet: Bohring-Opitz syndrome has previously been associated with high infant mortality (11 out of 43 reported patients (26%), intrauterine growth retardation, feeding difficulties often requiring a feeding tube, failure to thrive, severe to profound developmental delays, recurrent infections, nonspecific brain abnormalities, variable microcephaly, distinctive facial features

nodes.description: Hypertrichosis is a recurrent dysmorphic feature in BOS.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical management of patients with ASXL1 mutations and Bohring-Opitz syndrome, emphasizing the need for Wilms tumor surveillance.
evidence.snippet: Bohring-Opitz syndrome is a rare genetic condition characterized by distinctive facial features, variable microcephaly, hypertrichosis, nevus flammeus, severe myopia, unusual posture (flexion at the elbows with ulnar deviation, and flexion of the wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints), severe intellectual disability, and feeding issues.

nodes.description: Micrognathia is part of the characteristic BOS craniofacial gestalt.

evidence.snippet: The facial features may include microcephaly or trigonocephaly / prominent (but not fused) metopic ridge, hypotonic facies with full cheeks, synophrys, glabellar and eyelid nevus flammeus (simplex), prominent globes, widely set eyes, palate anomalies, and micrognathia.

nodes.description: Individuals with BOS appear to have an elevated risk of Wilms tumor, motivating surveillance in childhood.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical management of patients with ASXL1 mutations and Bohring-Opitz syndrome, emphasizing the need for Wilms tumor surveillance.
evidence.snippet: Including the patients discussed herein, there are 43 published patients with BOS of which two had Wilms tumor and one had bilateral nephroblastomatosis in infancy, making the incidence of a renal neoplastic process 7% among published patients, although this may be inflated due to biased ascertainment.

nodes.description: Glabellar and eyelid nevus flammeus is part of the characteristic BOS facial gestalt.

evidence.snippet: The facial features may include microcephaly or trigonocephaly / prominent (but not fused) metopic ridge, hypotonic facies with full cheeks, synophrys, glabellar and eyelid nevus flammeus (simplex), prominent globes, widely set eyes, palate anomalies, and micrognathia.

nodes.description: Severe intellectual disability is a defining BOS neurodevelopmental manifestation.

evidence.reference_title: De novo nonsense mutations in ASXL1 cause Bohring-Opitz syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Bohring-Opitz syndrome is characterized by severe intellectual disability, distinctive facial features and multiple congenital malformations.

nodes.description: Trigonocephaly or a prominent metopic ridge is a recurring cranial shape abnormality in BOS.

evidence.snippet: The facial features may include microcephaly or trigonocephaly / prominent (but not fused) metopic ridge, hypotonic facies with full cheeks, synophrys, glabellar and eyelid nevus flammeus (simplex), prominent globes, widely set eyes, palate anomalies, and micrognathia.
